sentences.text: My name is Liz Manning.
I was born Lizzie Sussmann.
My father's name was Deszoe-- David Sussmann.
The original name was based on a Hungarian man's name.
It's the capital D-E-S-Z-O with two dots on top of it.
My mother's name was Malvine von Sonnenfeld.
M-A-L-V-I-N-E.
I was born in Vienna, Austria, on January 4, 1926.
Actually, all my papers say that that I was born on January the 2nd, because there was a child born to my parents 11 months before I was born that only lived 5 days, a girl.
And when I was born, my father was so excited that he had now had a living child that looked and acted much healthier than the first one, that when he gave them the date, he made a mistake.
And I have had to live with this all my life.
All my papers said the second.
But since we were a religious family, and everybody knew that I was not born on Shabbat, but on a Monday, I knew that they were right, because I looked at the 1926 calendar.
And the fourth was a Monday.
The name of the child was named after a first cousin of my father's who had died two years-- a year before the first child.
Was Emma.
My father came from a small village in a province of Austria which is called Burgenland.
And my mother came from a place in Czechoslovakia, in Slovakia, what is now Slovakia.
It was called Dobra Voda.
And my father came from Lackenbach.
And Lackenbach was one of the communities that had been given by the then Austrian emperor, whoever it was, between 1500 and 1510 to a group of Jews from Spain that came north during the Inquisition.
And they founded seven communities, 100 families or 80 families to each.
And they lived there until they were made to leave in 1938 by Hitler, because they didn't want to have Jews anywhere in Austria except in Vienna.
My father's family had a very long history in Austria.
Also, there was one thing that I was very proud of, which my father told me when I was a little girl and he took me once to the museum to see it.
At the time of the Empress Maria Theresa, one of my ancestors had a letter of free passage.
That means Jews were not allowed to move more than a certain distance away from their place of domicile.
But he had a letter of free passage anywhere within the Austro-Hungarian empire.
He was free to travel, because he was a very, very learned man.
And he seemed to have performed some sort of service.
I don't know but it was.
There was no-- but the letter existed and was in the home museum of Eisenstadt, the capital of the province of Burgenland.
And when I was about eight years old, my father took me to see it and showed me.
He says, this is what you come from.
And I was very proud of it.
And after the war, I never managed to get back there, because there were too many corpses around.
And I wasn't able to make myself go back to Lackenbach.
I was only in Vienna once in 1983, a long time after.
But it was too hard.
And I didn't-- I'm sure they threw it out.
My parents were-- it was a arranged-- it was arranged marriage.
My mother was five years older.
My mother was at the time 33.
And my father was 28.
Her cousins lived across the street.
And my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father had had a relationship with a young lady who decided that he didn't-- she wanted to have a better life.
He wasn't rich enough.
So she married a furniture manufacturer.
And my father said, so all right, so if you can't marry-- I mean this was a story that were told to me afterwards-- if you can't marry for love, you marry for money.
And there was this young woman who was a cousin took across the street.
And the mother was well to do.
So she said she was going to give her a nice start.
And so they got married.
And when they came back from their honeymoon, he said, you know, he was going to set himself up in business.
He owned his own business.
But he was going to enlarge.
And he was a cabinet maker.
He made very fancy furniture.
He did realize, you know, that people would come in, somebody bought themselves a big house, a mansion, or something.
And he would design the furniture and make all the wood furniture.
He made upholstered furniture.
He designed beds.
And he was pretty good at what he was doing.
And so when he asked her for the money, she says, I can't give you my money.
She says, I'm an old woman.
What am I going to do?
I mean, what am I going to live on for the rest of my life?
So there was no-- and this was the situation after the war, after the First World War, was 1923.
And there was a very great shortage of apartments and thing.
If you didn't-- well, of course, they didn't have money right after the war.
He had been in the army and everything to buy their own home.
So she had a big apartment.
So they lived with her.
So my grandmother lived with us.
But my father never spoke to her again, ever.
She lived with us.
I was born in '26.
She lived in this place until we were able to buy our own in 1935.
And she went into an old age home by that time.
She was, what, 85 years old or something.
Her name was Regina Wollitzer, W-O-L-L-I-T-Z-E-R.
She came from a well-to-do family in Czechoslovakia.
And they had an estate.
And they were weaving their own clothes, excuse me, their own cloth.
And I still have dish towels that were woven for her dowry, my grandmother's dowry, that I still have with the R-W monogram in something called-- I don't know what it's called in English.
It's called Hanf in German, H-A-N-F, some sort of fiber.
That was my mother's mother.
And she lived with us.
She did not eat at the table with us.
She ate either before we ate or after we ate.
She lived in the same apartment.
They never had an argument.
They both acted as if the other one was not in existence.
And I can understand my father's feeling.
I mean, he had been had.
But he was a very good husband.
And he was a very good father.
And I adored him.
He was a very charming, charismatic man.
He was a physical specimen.
He would run around in snow storms with his sleeves rolled up.
He was very active in the Jewish soccer thing, Hakoah, the football team.
And he did weight lifting for fun.
Once or twice a week he went to a place and he did weightlifting.
That was his relaxation.
And my father had was born with a very beautiful tenor voice, a lyric tenor.
He had a very beautiful voice.
And, of course, his parents were in no condition to afford anything.
When they had a birthday, they got a soft boiled egg.
That was a sign that there was a birthday.
That's what their present.
He told me that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so when he was able to do this for himself, he went and took singing lessons to develop his voice.
And he was-- he came from a religious family and he was, you know, involved with religion in prayer.
So he became a chazzan.
And he was a cantor.
It was in Innsbruck.
And he was there for the high holy days.
And every year he would go there for the high holy days.
And that was his-- between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, that was his vacation.
As, you know, a fellow, that's what he was there.
He would go be a little bit mountain climbing and live there.

But he felt that the food that was being served to him wasn't as what he was used to from home, because one of my grandmother's sisters, the youngest, her name was Nina, had in her youth been a cook in the home of Count Esterhazy, who was a very famous Austrian nobleman.
And she could take two pieces of paper and make a banquet out of it.
So we ate the most fantastic food when I was a child.
And when I was older and I went to some restaurants in New York where the food costs more than somebody's salary.
And then you wind up and you have three peas and half a chicken leg on your plate, I realized that the food I ate in my own home was so much better than anything that I have ever eaten outside.
So when my parents got married, my mother had been the daughter of a rich home, and so she never knew how to cook.
So when she put the meat in front of him, my father said, so this is what I'm going to live on for the rest of my life.
So he took her and he packed her off to his mother in Lackenbach, you know, where she still lived.
And he says, teach her how to cook and don't send her home.
They got married on April 23rd.
Of what year?
And don't let her come home until she knows how to cook.
So she was there until Rosh Hashanah.
He came to visit on the weekends.
It wasn't far from Vienna.
But she was there.
And she didn't come back until Rosh Hashanah.
She was a very good cook.
So every morning when he was up in Innsbruck, she went down to the West Bannhof, which was the train that went to the west, the western railroad station.
And she gave the head of the dining car a market basket with food.
And that would be the express train, the fast train, to Innsbruck.
And my father would pick it up in the evening and then would give them the dishes from the next day.
And every morning, every morning early in the morning, my mother delivered the food.
My father didn't like to travel.
My father traveled like a gentleman.
He had a cane and a toothbrush.
And he didn't take anything with him.
He says, I can get it, and I don't need it.
And he's not traveling-- and my mother did that.
And we had a fantastic family life.
My father was one of six.
And they were on a very, very close family.
And they had children.
And they were constantly together.
Every Sunday afternoon, there was a dining room table which could seat, I don't know, 24,000-- 24 people.
I'm sorry, I'm getting carried away with myself. 20, 24 people.
It was a huge table with two leaves.
And it was a square thing.
And when it was opened there was no room except for chairs all around.
And there was this big expanse in the center.
We all got together.
And the men were playing a card game called scat and tarok, a German card game.
And the women were sitting in the kitchen and cooking all afternoon.
And we were always together, every Sunday afternoon.
And it was a wonderful life.
And every Mother's Day, we would all pack up and take a fresh goose and fresh peas and everything and would go down to Lackenbach and drop in on my grandmother.
She had a tiny little house.
It would probably fit into a one room studio efficiency.
Her name was Kathi, K-A-T-H-I, born Lederer, L-E-D-E-R-E-R.
My father's father died before I was born.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had a wagon.
And he went from market to market.
My grandmother was his second wife.
She was 27 when they got married.
And he was 54.
And they had six children together.
So everything seemed to have been OK.
The type of food that we ate, I would say, was strictly Hungarian.
It was very, very good food.
And I have very fond memories of everything.
And there was no man-made food in our house.
My mother made her own noodles.
She baked her own challah.
We bought bread.
But my father, we either ate goose fat or butter.
Everything was strictly kosher.
He wouldn't eat margarine.
He wouldn't eat oil.
He wouldn't-- everything was the very best.
And it was just the very best.
We had a wonderful life, a good family life.
Every Sunday morning I would go from aunt to aunt and collect my goodies.
Everybody knew I liked soup bones.
So they would all save the soup bones for me from Shabbat from the making the soup.
And I liked it.
I still do.
And they would save it for me.
And I'd go around.
And I would come home Sunday, and I never wanted to eat my lunch.
And it took years before my mother found out that they were feeding me.
I was collecting my pocket money.
That's what we did.
And my father had a sister.
She was an older sister.
Her name was Bertha, but everybody called her Tata because a niece that she had brought up, that was the first word she said.
And they developed a very strong bond between them.
And even her mother calls her Tata.
And her husband when she married later caller her.
She became Tata to all of us.
And she was a fantastically wonderful woman.
I'll tell you more about her later.
And I stayed with Tata when I was a little girl, because they had lost a child.
And there was a very bad winter in 1928.
And pipe water was freezing in the pipes and things.
But they were afraid something was going to happen to me.
And Tata lived with some of my grandmother's sisters and another aunt.
They had a huge apartment with inside rooms and outside the rooms.
So they took me up there.
And they were all worried-- I was only two years old-- that I was going to cry and be hysterical.
And they said that I would have to stay there because it was very cold, and in our place it was too cold, and I would be more comfortable there.
And when the flowers bloom and it gets warm outside I could come back.
So when my mother went home, she cried.
You know, she wanted-- she had to leave her baby.
They told me that, of course.
I don't remember it.
So they said, all right, we're going now.
So I lifted up my hand and I waved at her.
And I said, goodbye, Mommy-- in German, of course-- I see you when the flowers blooms.
And she never forgave me.
[LAUGHS] When I was 15 years old, she still told me that I just dismissed her.
I see you when the flowers bloom.
And then I take off.
And she says, I cried, but you didn't it.
I loved my aunt.
She had a business of making [INAUDIBLE].
She made embroidered bedding and blanket covers and night gowns and house coats and things like that.
They called it white sewing.
It wasn't clothing.
It was household articles and underwear, fancy slips and house coats and things like that.
And she had a business like that.
And that was long before sewing machine.
And I would sit for hours while she was sewing on her lap while she was doing that.
It was a very nice childhood.
We were all very close.
And my brother and I, we used to fight.
I had a younger brother who was born four years and three months after.
His name is Henry.
He lives today in Israel.
And we were fighting, like kids, you know.
And my father says, look at me and my six brothers and sisters, we get along.
And me with my big mouth, I was terrible, even as a child.
I would say, yeah, when we be as old as you are, we won't have anything to fight about any more.
Now, in fact, he is bothering me.
So it was a wonderful life.
And it was very nice.
And we would go down there for Mother's Day.
And the old people would fall in there, and the women would start cooking up a storm.
And then we would eat in the garden a meal.
And at night, we would all come home at 10 o'clock, at night at 11 o'clock at night, totally stuffed to the gills and very happy and sleepy.
Did you ever go with your father to Innsbruck?
No.
Don't forget, he told us about this.
He brought home grapes from Merano in Italy, which were black grapes, which were the size of walnuts.
But that was his time.
He was the cantor of this community.
He was involved.
He lived in somebody's house.
It wasn't a family vacation.
Besides that I had to be-- it was too early.
If I would have been a teenager, I'm sure I would have been able to go.
I was only 12 years old when Hitler came to Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It just didn't lend itself.
I most certainly do.
My father had a very beautiful voice.
Unfortunately the way the world was made and everything, we didn't have anything-- I had made a tape, but that was the summer before he died.
I have [INAUDIBLE] and a few Austrian folk songs.
But it was still far better than any other amateur.
But it wasn't-- the soaring, fabulous.
You know, it was so clear.
I was nine years old, I could tell when tenor was singing like he had dumplings in his throat.
You know, there's an expression when somebody sings like that in German.
I could tell because I was used to this clear singing.
I had a very beautiful voice too.
And when I was about 10 years old, the teacher called my father to school.
And she wanted to know if he was going to have the voice developed.
And he said, no, she wants to study medicine.
She wants to be a doctor.
She wants to cut people.
She wants to be a surgeon.
And she said, it is a gift that very few people had, that she we should do something about it.
Unfortunately, there was never any choice.
Two years later the world came apart at the seams.
And that was the end of that.
And I was sitting in back of a truck in the snow and singing on top of my lungs and the voice went.
But when I got married I still could reach a high C without any effort.
But, of course, I never learned how to sing and breathe or anything.
I have wanted to study medicine since I've been four years old.
I had an old lady friend who owned the chickens-- my mother used to buy chicken stand.
I made friends with the old lady.
And I went there, and I watched her open up the chickens.
I was fascinated.
And then I found another friend, Fraulein Klinger.
She was a spinster daughter of our butcher.
And I would spend hours standing after school in the afternoon in her store watching her taking apart the veal carcasses.
The cows were a little bit too big for me.
I was intimidated.
But the veal carcasses, you know, the calf, I was watching.
I was fascinated by the inside.
And I wanted to study medicine because I wanted to do this.
And when I got older, I know this sounds sort of presumptuous, but I still feel it.
I think being as-- so I make a fool of myself, but that's what I think.

Being a surgeon is the most unbelievably fascinating thing a human being can become, because it's the closest thing somebody can do of being to a spirit-- a human can come of being God, because you work on living tissue that is broken and you fix it.
And it gets well and the person lives better than they did before.
And I think this is a fabulous profession.
And that's what I wanted to do all my life.
And it was denied me.
And I'm still dreaming about the that I always thought-- I read for bedtime reading.
I used to be it many, many years ago.
And I watched every heart operation every chance that I have.
I watch open surgery.
Once I was able to watch one from up in the gallery, or whatever you call that.
And it was the most unbelievable thing-- outside of seeing Jerusalem for the first time-- that was the biggest thing that ever happened to me in my whole life.
I'm still absolutely, totally involved in it.
But it never worked out.
And I was going to go back after I retired.
And then when I retired, I realized that I didn't have the fire in the belly anymore.
I didn't want to give it the time.
I would've been able to get into any school with my history, camps and everything.
But I didn't want to.
As a child, I was hooked up on Egyptian-- on the old Egyptians.
I love to read books about the pyramids, because I said my ancestors built them.
And it took me until 1991 before I was able to see them.
But they did a good job.
They did a good job.
But I was very, very fascinated, because the world around them was so primitive, especially Europe, you know, with bear skins and clubs.
And they were doing all of these things.
And I was very fascinated by that.
I always had a very, very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inquisitive mind.
I wanted to know how everything-- I wanted to know how it was.
And even if I didn't understand it, I wanted to know.
And maybe four years later, if I could figure out how it worked out, very well.
I'm still that way.
Even if I don't know what it's like, I want to know what it is.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the childhood was very nice.
And I was in school.
And I had a big mouth.
You want me to tell you one of my-- I want to give you an express-- a thing of what my father.
My father was a very religious man.
If I had said some of the things to my father that my children said to me-- and they were never disrespectful.
I have wonderful children-- I wouldn't be sitting here.
I mean, this is an exaggeration, of course.
But figuratively speaking, I wouldn't be sitting here talking to you.
But my father was a very special man.
He was fair.
He wasn't a dictator.
It was just he says, I make the rules.
Those are the rules.
If you live by the rules, everything is fine.
And I always used to say when I got older, he's got to be right or God help the world, he'll take it apart.
Well, I wasn't very respect-- I had a big mouth.
Anyway, so one day I was reading a book about the old Indians, about the American Indians in school.
And the teacher was going on about something.
And I didn't want to-- I must have been about nine or ten-- was going on about something.
And I didn't want.
It was boring and I knew anyway.
I read the book already.
And I didn't--
So I had this thing on my lap, the book.
And I was looking down and reading.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said to me, I should stop reading, and I should pay attention.
And I said, I'm not reading.
And she said, yes, you are.
And I said, no, I'm not.
By that time the book was already in my school bag, and it was no longer in my lap.
I figured if she'd come back.
Anyway, so she said, yes, I know.
I said, well, you have an academic education, you should know better than I do.
So she was shocked.
She called my father to school.
And my father came to school.
And she told him that I had said that, you know, this little incident.
And he said, was she disrespectful?
She said, no, she said you have an academic education and you should know much better than I do.
He says, do you have an academic education?
She said, of course I do.
You know that.
He said, why are you angry at the child? she told you the truth.
So that was my father.
What kind of schooling did you have?
I didn't have any schooling.
I went to an elementary school, which lasted until-- well, the elementary school in Vienna at the time-- still is-- was four years.
Yeah.
It didn't have a name.
It was in a street, which was called Treustrasse.
And I went to the school in Treustrasee, which was about two blocks from my house.
And we went there.
And it was an all girls school.
Girls and boys didn't all go to school together in Vienna.
And then at 10, I went to an eight-year high school.
It was called the gymnasium.
And from there you could get into college.
But I only went there for a year and a half, because in 1938 all the Jewish children were told that they could no longer go to public school.
And that was my education.
And the rest was home made.
Oh, yes, I did have Jewish education.
My father was very religious.
But he was special.
He said, God gave her a brain even though she was a girl.
Let her use it.
And he allowed me to go to school.
I went to a place for girls, which was called Beth Jakov and we learned [?
Komisch ?] as a girl, which was very, very unusual in the 1930s in Europe in a religious home.
It was very unusual.
But there were other girls, too, so there must have been a few other enlightened parents in the world.
And I also went to a Hebrew school where I learned modern Hebrew.
And when I was 12 years old, you know, when Hitler came, I spoke beautiful Hebrew.
And when I went to Israel for the first time in 1970, I could always read, of course.
I knew all the nouns, but I had forgotten all the connecting words-- by, to, from, with, at.
So I was sitting in the taxi going from the airport to Tel Aviv.
And I couldn't sit still, because it had been so long, I don't go-- I don't pray, and I don't go to synagogue.
And I had no had no connection with religion.
We're just going to have to pause for a second to change the tape.
All right.
